items.title: Countrywise Withholding Tax Rates / Chart as per DTAA
items.summary: The applicable withholding tax depends on the Income-tax Act or the relevant DTAA, whichever is more beneficial. Treaty rates differ across countries and income categories such as dividends, interest, royalties and FTS. The post Countrywise Withholding Tax Rates / Chart as per DTAA appeared first on TaxGuru .
items.body:
Background & Statutory Framework

The concept of withholding tax is rooted in Section 195 of the Income Tax Act, 1961, which mandates the deduction of tax at source on certain payments made to non-residents. The rate of withholding tax is determined by the provisions of the Income Tax Act or the applicable Double Taxation Avoidance Agreement (DTAA), whichever is more beneficial to the taxpayer. The DTAAs are negotiated between countries to prevent double taxation and fiscal evasion, and they play a crucial role in determining the withholding tax rates. For instance, Section 90 of the Income Tax Act allows taxpayers to claim the benefits of a DTAA, provided they furnish the required documentation and meet the specified conditions.

The withholding tax rates vary across countries and income categories, such as dividends, interest, royalties, and fees for technical services (FTS). The rates are specified in the DTAA and are often lower than the rates prescribed under the Income Tax Act. For example, the DTAA between India and the United States prescribes a withholding tax rate of 15% on dividends, whereas the Income Tax Act prescribes a rate of 20%. Similarly, the DTAA between India and the United Kingdom prescribes a withholding tax rate of 10% on interest, whereas the Income Tax Act prescribes a rate of 20%.

The CBDT has issued various circulars and notifications to clarify the withholding tax provisions, including Circular No. 789/2000, which provides guidance on the withholding tax rates applicable to non-resident taxpayers. Additionally, the Supreme Court has pronounced several judgments on the interpretation of DTAAs, including the landmark case of Union of India vs. Azadi Bachao Andolan (2003), which held that the provisions of a DTAA would override the provisions of the Income Tax Act in cases of conflict.

The Development in Detail

The countrywise withholding tax rates chart, as per the DTAA, is a comprehensive guide that outlines the withholding tax rates applicable to different countries and income categories. The chart is based on the provisions of the DTAAs negotiated between India and various countries, including the United States, the United Kingdom, Canada, and Australia. The chart provides a ready reckoner for taxpayers and tax practitioners to determine the applicable withholding tax rates and ensure compliance with the provisions of the Income Tax Act and the DTAAs.

The chart is divided into several categories, including dividends, interest, royalties, and FTS. Each category has a separate table that outlines the withholding tax rates applicable to different countries. For example, the table for dividends outlines the withholding tax rates applicable to dividends paid to non-resident shareholders, including the rates prescribed under the DTAA and the Income Tax Act. Similarly, the table for interest outlines the withholding tax rates applicable to interest paid to non-resident lenders, including the rates prescribed under the DTAA and the Income Tax Act.

The chart also provides guidance on the documentation requirements and the procedures for claiming the benefits of a DTAA. For instance, it outlines the requirements for obtaining a tax residency certificate from the tax authorities of the country of residence, which is a necessary document for claiming the benefits of a DTAA. Additionally, the chart provides guidance on the procedures for filing the withholding tax returns and the payment of withholding tax, including the due dates and the consequences of non-compliance.

Key Provisions & Impact

The countrywise withholding tax rates chart, as per the DTAA, has significant implications for taxpayers and tax practitioners. The chart provides clarity on the withholding tax rates applicable to different countries and income categories, which helps to avoid errors and ensures compliance with the provisions of the Income Tax Act and the DTAAs. For example, a non-resident taxpayer receiving dividends from an Indian company can use the chart to determine the applicable withholding tax rate and ensure that the correct amount of tax is deducted at source.

The chart also helps to reduce the risk of double taxation, which can arise when a taxpayer is taxed on the same income in two or more countries. By providing clarity on the withholding tax rates and the procedures for claiming the benefits of a DTAA, the chart helps to prevent double taxation and ensures that taxpayers are not unfairly taxed. Additionally, the chart helps to promote cross-border trade and investment by providing a clear and transparent framework for the taxation of international transactions.

However, the chart also highlights the complexity of the withholding tax provisions and the need for careful planning and compliance. Taxpayers and tax practitioners must carefully review the chart and the underlying provisions of the Income Tax Act and the DTAAs to ensure that they are in compliance with the applicable laws and regulations. Failure to comply with the withholding tax provisions can result in significant penalties and interest, including the imposition of a penalty of up to 100% of the tax amount under Section 271C of the Income Tax Act.

Critical Analysis

The countrywise withholding tax rates chart, as per the DTAA, is a valuable resource for taxpayers and tax practitioners. However, it is not without its limitations. The chart is based on the provisions of the DTAAs, which are subject to change and may not reflect the current tax laws and regulations of the respective countries. Additionally, the chart may not provide comprehensive coverage of all the countries and income categories, which can create uncertainty and ambiguity.

Furthermore, the chart highlights the need for a more streamlined and simplified approach to withholding tax. The current system is complex and cumbersome, with multiple rates and procedures applicable to different countries and income categories. A more streamlined approach would help to reduce the compliance burden and promote cross-border trade and investment. For example, the government could consider introducing a single withholding tax rate applicable to all non-resident taxpayers, which would simplify the compliance procedures and reduce the risk of errors.

Additionally, the chart underscores the importance of tax treaty negotiations and the need for India to renegotiate its DTAAs with other countries. The current DTAAs are often outdated and do not reflect the current economic and tax realities. Renegotiating the DTAAs would help to promote cross-border trade and investment and ensure that India remains a competitive destination for foreign investment. For instance, the government could consider renegotiating the DTAA with the United States to reduce the withholding tax rate on dividends and interest, which would promote cross-border investment and trade between the two countries.

Compliance Implications & Action Points

The countrywise withholding tax rates chart, as per the DTAA, has significant compliance implications for taxpayers and tax practitioners. To ensure compliance with the withholding tax provisions, taxpayers and tax practitioners must carefully review the chart and the underlying provisions of the Income Tax Act and the DTAAs. Here are some key takeaways for practitioners:

1. Review the countrywise withholding tax rates chart to determine the applicable withholding tax rate for each country and income category.

2. Ensure that the correct amount of tax is deducted at source and that the withholding tax returns are filed on time to avoid penalties and interest.

3. Obtain a tax residency certificate from the tax authorities of the country of residence to claim the benefits of a DTAA.

4. Maintain accurate and detailed documentation, including the withholding tax returns and the tax residency certificate, to support the claim for the benefits of a DTAA.

5. Consider seeking professional advice from a tax expert or a chartered accountant to ensure compliance with the withholding tax provisions and to minimize the risk of errors and penalties.

items.title: Special Tax Rates for Capital Gains/Incomes arising out of certain securities
items.summary: The document provides a detailed summary of the special tax provisions applicable to different securities and classes of taxpayers. It explains the applicable rates for capital gains and specified income along with deduction and exemption rules under the Finance Act, 2026. The post Special Tax Rates for Capital Gains/Incomes arising out of certain securities appeared first on TaxGuru .
items.body:
Background & Statutory Framework

The Indian tax landscape has witnessed significant developments in recent years, with a focus on simplifying the tax regime and promoting ease of doing business. The Finance Act, 2026, has introduced special tax provisions applicable to different securities and classes of taxpayers. These provisions aim to provide clarity and certainty on the tax treatment of capital gains and specified income arising from certain securities. The Income Tax Act, 1961, particularly Section 45, which deals with capital gains, and Section 56, which deals with income from other sources, are relevant in this context. Additionally, Section 10 of the Income Tax Act, which provides for exemptions, is also crucial in understanding the special tax provisions.

The concept of special tax rates for capital gains and specified income is not new in India. The government has been introducing various provisions to incentivize investment in specific sectors, such as infrastructure and startups. For instance, Section 54EC of the Income Tax Act provides for an exemption from capital gains tax if the gains are invested in certain bonds. Similarly, Section 54GB provides for an exemption from capital gains tax if the gains are invested in a startup. These provisions demonstrate the government's intent to promote investment in specific sectors and provide relief to taxpayers.

The CBDT has also issued circulars and notifications to provide clarity on the tax treatment of capital gains and specified income. For example, Circular No. 12/2016, dated June 15, 2016, provides guidance on the tax treatment of capital gains arising from the transfer of securities. These circulars and notifications are essential in understanding the special tax provisions introduced by the Finance Act, 2026.

The Development in Detail

The Finance Act, 2026, has introduced special tax provisions applicable to different securities and classes of taxpayers. These provisions aim to provide clarity and certainty on the tax treatment of capital gains and specified income arising from certain securities. The Act has introduced a new Section 112A, which deals with the tax treatment of capital gains arising from the transfer of securities. This section provides for a special tax rate of 10% on long-term capital gains exceeding Rs. 1 lakh. The section also provides for a deduction of 50% of the capital gains, which can be claimed as a deduction under Section 80C.

The Act has also introduced a new Section 115BBDA, which deals with the tax treatment of specified income arising from certain securities. This section provides for a special tax rate of 10% on specified income exceeding Rs. 10 lakh. The section also provides for a deduction of 25% of the specified income, which can be claimed as a deduction under Section 80C. These provisions are expected to provide relief to taxpayers and promote investment in specific sectors.

The CBDT has also issued a notification, dated March 15, 2026, which provides guidance on the implementation of the special tax provisions introduced by the Finance Act, 2026. The notification provides clarity on the tax treatment of capital gains and specified income arising from certain securities and is essential in understanding the practical implications of the special tax provisions.

Key Provisions & Impact

The special tax provisions introduced by the Finance Act, 2026, are expected to have a significant impact on taxpayers. The provisions aim to provide clarity and certainty on the tax treatment of capital gains and specified income arising from certain securities. The special tax rate of 10% on long-term capital gains exceeding Rs. 1 lakh is expected to provide relief to taxpayers who have invested in securities for the long term. Similarly, the special tax rate of 10% on specified income exceeding Rs. 10 lakh is expected to provide relief to taxpayers who have earned specified income from certain securities.

The provisions are also expected to promote investment in specific sectors, such as infrastructure and startups. The deduction of 50% of the capital gains, which can be claimed as a deduction under Section 80C, is expected to incentivize taxpayers to invest in securities for the long term. Similarly, the deduction of 25% of the specified income, which can be claimed as a deduction under Section 80C, is expected to incentivize taxpayers to earn specified income from certain securities.

The provisions are also expected to have a significant impact on the tax administration. The CBDT will need to issue guidelines and circulars to provide clarity on the implementation of the special tax provisions. The tax authorities will also need to ensure that taxpayers comply with the provisions and claim the deductions and exemptions correctly. The provisions are expected to reduce litigation and provide clarity on the tax treatment of capital gains and specified income arising from certain securities.

Critical Analysis

The special tax provisions introduced by the Finance Act, 2026, are a welcome move towards providing clarity and certainty on the tax treatment of capital gains and specified income arising from certain securities. The provisions are expected to provide relief to taxpayers and promote investment in specific sectors. However, the provisions also raise certain concerns, such as the potential for tax avoidance and the complexity of the provisions.

The provisions may be subject to interpretation, and taxpayers may need to seek guidance from tax professionals to ensure compliance. The CBDT will need to issue guidelines and circulars to provide clarity on the implementation of the provisions. The tax authorities will also need to ensure that taxpayers comply with the provisions and claim the deductions and exemptions correctly.

The provisions may also have a significant impact on the tax revenue. The special tax rate of 10% on long-term capital gains exceeding Rs. 1 lakh and the special tax rate of 10% on specified income exceeding Rs. 10 lakh may reduce the tax revenue. However, the provisions are expected to promote investment in specific sectors and provide relief to taxpayers, which may offset the reduction in tax revenue.

Compliance Implications & Action Points

The special tax provisions introduced by the Finance Act, 2026, have significant compliance implications for taxpayers. Taxpayers will need to ensure that they comply with the provisions and claim the deductions and exemptions correctly. The following are some key action points for practitioners:

1. Review the tax treatment of capital gains and specified income arising from certain securities to ensure compliance with the special tax provisions.

2. Claim the deductions and exemptions correctly under Section 80C to ensure that taxpayers receive the benefits of the special tax provisions.

3. Ensure that taxpayers maintain accurate records and documentation to support the claims made under the special tax provisions.

4. Seek guidance from tax professionals to ensure compliance with the provisions and to address any concerns or queries that taxpayers may have.

5. Monitor the CBDT guidelines and circulars to ensure that taxpayers are aware of any changes or updates to the special tax provisions.

items.title: Residence in India for the purpose of Income-tax Act, 1961
items.summary: The Income-tax Act determines residential status based on an individual's stay in India, income, citizenship and tax liability abroad. Different rules also apply to HUFs, companies, firms and other entities. The post Residence in India for the purpose of Income-tax Act, 1961 appeared first on TaxGuru .
items.body:
Background & Statutory Framework

The concept of residence plays a crucial role in determining the tax liability of an individual or entity under the Income-tax Act, 1961. The Act prescribes different rules for determining the residential status of various entities, including individuals, Hindu Undivided Families (HUFs), companies, firms, and other entities. For individuals, the residential status is determined based on their physical presence in India, income, citizenship, and tax liability abroad. Section 6 of the Income-tax Act, 1961, lays down the conditions for an individual to be considered a resident in India. An individual is considered a resident in India if they satisfy any of the following conditions: they are in India for at least 182 days in the previous year, or their income is sourced from India, or they have a permanent home in India and have been in India for at least 60 days in the previous year.

The residential status of an individual is significant because it determines their tax liability in India. A resident individual is taxed on their global income, whereas a non-resident individual is taxed only on their Indian-sourced income. The Act also provides for a special category of residents, known as "not ordinarily resident" (NOR), who are taxed on their Indian-sourced income and foreign-sourced income that is brought into India. Section 6(6) of the Act defines an NOR as an individual who has been a non-resident in India for at least 9 out of 10 preceding years or has been in India for at least 730 days in the preceding 7 years.

The residential status of other entities, such as HUFs, companies, and firms, is also determined based on specific rules. For example, a HUF is considered a resident in India if its control and management are situated wholly or partly in India. A company is considered a resident in India if it is an Indian company, as defined under the Companies Act, 2013. The residential status of these entities is significant because it determines their tax liability and the applicability of various tax laws and regulations.

The Development in Detail

The Income-tax Act, 1961, has undergone several amendments over the years to clarify and modify the rules for determining the residential status of individuals and other entities. One of the significant amendments was the introduction of Section 6(1A) of the Act, which provides that an individual who is a citizen of India and is not a tax resident in any other country shall be deemed to be a resident in India. This amendment was introduced to prevent individuals from avoiding tax by claiming to be non-residents in India.

Another significant development is the introduction of the concept of "significant economic presence" (SEP) in India. The Finance Act, 2018, inserted Section 9(1)(i) of the Act, which provides that an income that arises from transactions where the aggregate payment made by an Indian resident exceeds INR 20 million in a financial year shall be deemed to be income that is sourced in India. This provision is aimed at taxing foreign companies that have a significant economic presence in India but do not have a physical presence.

The Central Board of Direct Taxes (CBDT) has also issued several circulars and notifications to clarify the rules for determining the residential status of individuals and other entities. For example, Circular No. 2 of 2019, dated January 17, 2019, provides guidance on the determination of the residential status of an individual who is a citizen of India and is working abroad. The circular clarifies that an individual who is a citizen of India and is working abroad shall be considered a non-resident in India if they satisfy certain conditions, such as being outside India for at least 182 days in the previous year.

Key Provisions & Impact

The residential status of an individual or entity has a significant impact on their tax liability in India. A resident individual is taxed on their global income, whereas a non-resident individual is taxed only on their Indian-sourced income. The Act provides for a tax rate of 30% for resident individuals, whereas the tax rate for non-resident individuals is 20% for Indian-sourced income. The residential status of an entity, such as a HUF or a company, also determines its tax liability and the applicability of various tax laws and regulations.

Section 90 of the Act provides for a tax credit to a resident individual who has paid tax on their foreign-sourced income in a foreign country. The tax credit is allowed to avoid double taxation of the same income. The Act also provides for a foreign tax credit to a non-resident individual who has paid tax on their Indian-sourced income in a foreign country.

The residential status of an individual or entity also has an impact on their compliance obligations under the Act. A resident individual is required to file a tax return in India if their income exceeds the basic exemption limit, whereas a non-resident individual is required to file a tax return only if they have Indian-sourced income. The Act also requires a resident entity to file a tax return if its income exceeds the basic exemption limit.

Critical Analysis

The rules for determining the residential status of individuals and entities under the Income-tax Act, 1961, are complex and require careful analysis. The Act provides for different rules for different entities, and the residential status of an entity can have a significant impact on its tax liability and compliance obligations. The introduction of the concept of SEP in India is a significant development that aims to tax foreign companies that have a significant economic presence in India but do not have a physical presence.

However, the rules for determining the residential status of individuals and entities are often subject to interpretation, and there may be disputes between the taxpayer and the tax authorities. The CBDT has issued several circulars and notifications to clarify the rules, but there is still a need for further clarification and guidance. The Act also requires a taxpayer to maintain various documents and records to support their residential status, and failure to do so can result in penalties and fines.

The residential status of an individual or entity is also significant from a tax planning perspective. A taxpayer can plan their tax affairs to minimize their tax liability by taking advantage of the rules for determining the residential status. For example, an individual who is a resident in India can plan their foreign-sourced income to avoid tax in India by taking advantage of the tax credit provisions under the Act.

Compliance Implications & Action Points

The residential status of an individual or entity has significant compliance implications under the Income-tax Act, 1961. A taxpayer must ensure that they comply with the rules for determining their residential status to avoid penalties and fines. Here are some key takeaways for practitioners:

1. Determine the residential status of an individual or entity based on the rules under the Act, and ensure that all necessary documents and records are maintained to support the residential status.

2. Ensure that a tax return is filed in India if the income of an individual or entity exceeds the basic exemption limit, and claim a tax credit for foreign-sourced income if eligible.

3. Maintain records of foreign-sourced income and tax paid in a foreign country to claim a tax credit under the Act.

4. Ensure that all necessary compliance obligations are met, such as obtaining a permanent account number (PAN) and filing a tax return, to avoid penalties and fines.

5. Plan tax affairs to minimize tax liability by taking advantage of the rules for determining the residential status, such as claiming a tax credit for foreign-sourced income or taking advantage of the SEP provisions under the Act.

items.title: Paying electricity bills with a credit card? Here’s how to maximise cashback credit card rewards
items.summary: Electricity bill payments can earn cashback if the credit card rewards utility transactions. Choosing the right card and paying bills on time helps maximise savings. The post Paying electricity bills with a credit card? Here’s how to maximise cashback credit card rewards appeared first on TaxGuru .
items.body:
Background & Statutory Framework

The payment of electricity bills using credit cards has become a common practice, with many individuals and businesses opting for this mode of payment due to its convenience and potential rewards. From a tax perspective, the payment of electricity bills is considered a deductible expense under Section 30 of the Income Tax Act, 1961, which allows for the deduction of rent, rates, and taxes in respect of a building occupied for business purposes. However, the use of credit cards to pay electricity bills also raises questions about the applicability of cashback rewards and their treatment under the tax laws. Section 28 of the Income Tax Act, 1961, which deals with the income from other sources, may also be relevant in this context, as cashback rewards could potentially be considered as income from other sources.

The Central Board of Direct Taxes (CBDT) has issued various circulars and notifications regarding the taxation of cashback rewards and other forms of income from credit card transactions. For instance, Circular No. 22/2017, dated June 26, 2017, provides guidance on the taxation of cashback rewards and discounts offered by e-commerce companies. Similarly, the Goods and Services Tax (GST) laws also provide for the taxation of services related to the payment of electricity bills, including the services provided by credit card companies. Section 9(1) of the CGST Act, 2017, which deals with the levy and collection of GST, may be relevant in this context.

The Supreme Court has also dealt with the issue of cashback rewards and their taxation in various cases. For example, in the case of CIT vs. Hindustan Lever Ltd., the Court held that cashback rewards paid to distributors were taxable as business income. Similarly, in the case of CIT vs. LG Electronics India Pvt. Ltd., the Court held that cashback rewards paid to customers were taxable as income from other sources. These judgments provide valuable guidance on the taxation of cashback rewards and their treatment under the tax laws.

The Development in Detail

The recent development regarding the payment of electricity bills using credit cards and the potential for cashback rewards has significant implications for taxpayers. Many credit card companies offer cashback rewards on utility transactions, including electricity bill payments. By choosing the right credit card and paying electricity bills on time, taxpayers can maximize their cashback rewards and reduce their tax liability. However, it is essential to understand the terms and conditions of the credit card and the cashback rewards program to ensure that the rewards are maximized and the tax implications are minimized.

The payment of electricity bills using credit cards also raises questions about the applicability of Section 40(a)(ia) of the Income Tax Act, 1961, which deals with the disallowance of expenses for non-deduction of tax at source. If the credit card company is required to deduct tax at source on the cashback rewards, and fails to do so, the taxpayer may be liable for the disallowance of the expense. Therefore, it is crucial to ensure that the credit card company complies with the tax deduction at source requirements to avoid any disallowance of expenses.

Furthermore, the payment of electricity bills using credit cards may also be subject to GST, depending on the nature of the services provided by the credit card company. Section 2(17) of the CGST Act, 2017, defines the term "business" to include any activity carried out by a person, whether for profit or not. If the credit card company provides services related to the payment of electricity bills, including the collection of payments and the provision of cashback rewards, these services may be subject to GST. Therefore, taxpayers must ensure that they comply with the GST laws and regulations to avoid any GST liability.

Key Provisions & Impact

The key provisions of the Income Tax Act, 1961, and the CGST Act, 2017, have a significant impact on the payment of electricity bills using credit cards and the potential for cashback rewards. Section 80C of the Income Tax Act, 1961, which deals with the deduction of certain expenses, may be relevant in this context, as the payment of electricity bills may be considered as a deductible expense. However, the cashback rewards may be taxable as income from other sources under Section 28 of the Income Tax Act, 1961.

The GST laws also have an impact on the payment of electricity bills using credit cards. Section 15 of the CGST Act, 2017, which deals with the value of supply, may be relevant in this context, as the value of the supply of electricity may be subject to GST. Furthermore, Section 20 of the CGST Act, 2017, which deals with the liability to pay GST, may also be relevant, as the credit card company may be liable to pay GST on the services provided.

The payment of electricity bills using credit cards also raises questions about the applicability of the Black Money (Undisclosed Foreign Income and Assets) and Imposition of Tax Act, 2015. Section 2 of the Act defines the term "undisclosed foreign income" to include any income that is not disclosed in the return of income. If the cashback rewards are considered as income from other sources, and are not disclosed in the return of income, the taxpayer may be liable for the imposition of tax and penalty under the Act.

Critical Analysis

A critical analysis of the payment of electricity bills using credit cards and the potential for cashback rewards reveals that there are several tax implications that taxpayers must consider. The cashback rewards may be taxable as income from other sources, and the payment of electricity bills may be subject to GST. Furthermore, the credit card company may be liable to pay GST on the services provided, and the taxpayer may be liable for the disallowance of expenses if the credit card company fails to deduct tax at source.

However, the payment of electricity bills using credit cards also offers several benefits, including convenience, cashback rewards, and the potential for tax savings. By choosing the right credit card and paying electricity bills on time, taxpayers can maximize their cashback rewards and reduce their tax liability. Therefore, it is essential for taxpayers to understand the terms and conditions of the credit card and the cashback rewards program to ensure that the rewards are maximized and the tax implications are minimized.

The CBDT and the GST Council must also provide clarity on the tax implications of the payment of electricity bills using credit cards and the potential for cashback rewards. The issuance of circulars and notifications can help to provide guidance on the taxation of cashback rewards and the applicability of GST to the services provided by credit card companies. This can help to reduce the uncertainty and complexity surrounding the payment of electricity bills using credit cards and the potential for cashback rewards.

Compliance Implications & Action Points

The payment of electricity bills using credit cards and the potential for cashback rewards has significant compliance implications for taxpayers. To ensure compliance with the tax laws and regulations, taxpayers must take the following action points:

1. Choose the right credit card: Taxpayers must choose a credit card that offers cashback rewards on utility transactions, including electricity bill payments. They must also ensure that the credit card company complies with the tax deduction at source requirements to avoid any disallowance of expenses.

2. Pay electricity bills on time: Taxpayers must pay their electricity bills on time to maximize their cashback rewards and reduce their tax liability. They must also ensure that the payment of electricity bills is made through a legitimate channel, such as the credit card company's website or mobile app.

3. Maintain proper documentation: Taxpayers must maintain proper documentation, including the credit card statement, the electricity bill, and the cashback rewards statement, to support their claim for cashback rewards and to comply with the tax laws and regulations.

4. Disclose cashback rewards in the return of income: Taxpayers must disclose the cashback rewards in their return of income to avoid any penalty or interest for non-disclosure. They must also ensure that the cashback rewards are properly accounted for in their books of accounts.

5. Comply with GST laws and regulations: Taxpayers must comply with the GST laws and regulations, including the payment of GST on the services provided by the credit card company. They must also ensure that the credit card company complies with the GST laws and regulations to avoid any GST liability.

items.title: No disallowance of quantified liabilities, documented payments & reconciled books on a purely ad hoc basis

items.title: CBIC Revises Tariff Values for Palm/Soybean Oil, Silver & Brass Scrap from 30.06.2026
items.summary: Notification No. 59/2026-Customs (N.T.) substitutes the tariff value tables while retaining the existing customs tariff values. The revised tables become effective from 30 June 2026 without changing valuation rates. The post CBIC Revises Tariff Values for Palm/Soybean Oil, Silver & Brass Scrap from 30.06.2026 appeared first on TaxGuru .
items.body:
Background & Statutory Framework

The Central Board of Indirect Taxes and Customs (CBIC) has recently issued Notification No. 59/2026-Customs (N.T.), revising the tariff values for certain commodities, including palm/soybean oil, silver, and brass scrap, effective from 30 June 2026. This move is in exercise of the powers conferred by Section 14 of the Customs Act, 1962, which empowers the Central Government to fix tariff values for any class of imported or export goods. The revision of tariff values is a critical aspect of customs law, as it directly impacts the valuation of goods for the purpose of levying customs duty, and consequently, the overall cost of imports.

Section 14 of the Customs Act, 1962, read with Section 2(11) of the Customs Tariff Act, 1975, provides the statutory framework for the determination of tariff values. The CBIC, as the apex body responsible for the administration of customs law, is empowered to notify tariff values for various commodities from time to time. These notifications are issued in the public interest, taking into account factors such as international market prices, supply and demand, and other relevant considerations. The revision of tariff values is a dynamic process, reflecting changes in global market trends and the need to maintain a level playing field for domestic industries.

In the context of the present notification, it is relevant to note that the CBIC has retained the existing customs tariff values, while revising the tariff value tables. This approach is consistent with the principles of customs law, which aim to strike a balance between the need to protect domestic industries and the need to facilitate international trade. The retention of existing customs tariff values ensures continuity and stability in the customs regime, while the revision of tariff value tables reflects the CBIC's efforts to keep pace with changing market realities.

The Development in Detail

The Notification No. 59/2026-Customs (N.T.) substitutes the tariff value tables for palm/soybean oil, silver, and brass scrap, effective from 30 June 2026. The revised tables are appended to the notification, providing specific tariff values for each commodity. The notification also clarifies that the existing customs tariff values for these commodities will continue to apply, and the revision of tariff value tables will not affect the overall valuation of goods for customs purposes.

The revision of tariff values is a significant development, as it has a direct bearing on the customs duty payable on imported goods. The tariff value of a commodity is a critical factor in determining the assessable value of goods, which, in turn, affects the amount of customs duty leviable. The CBIC's decision to revise the tariff value tables for palm/soybean oil, silver, and brass scrap reflects its efforts to ensure that the customs regime remains aligned with international market trends and the needs of domestic industries.

It is also relevant to note that the notification has been issued in exercise of the powers conferred by Section 14 of the Customs Act, 1962, which empowers the Central Government to fix tariff values for any class of imported or export goods. This provision is a key aspect of customs law, as it enables the government to respond to changing market conditions and maintain a flexible customs regime. The CBIC's use of this provision to revise the tariff value tables for palm/soybean oil, silver, and brass scrap demonstrates its commitment to ensuring that the customs regime remains responsive to the needs of trade and industry.

Key Provisions & Impact

The Notification No. 59/2026-Customs (N.T.) has significant implications for importers and exporters of palm/soybean oil, silver, and brass scrap. The revision of tariff value tables will affect the valuation of these commodities for customs purposes, and consequently, the amount of customs duty payable. The notification also has implications for the overall customs regime, as it reflects the CBIC's efforts to maintain a level playing field for domestic industries and facilitate international trade.

The key provisions of the notification include the substitution of the tariff value tables for palm/soybean oil, silver, and brass scrap, effective from 30 June 2026. The notification also clarifies that the existing customs tariff values for these commodities will continue to apply, and the revision of tariff value tables will not affect the overall valuation of goods for customs purposes. The CBIC's decision to retain the existing customs tariff values while revising the tariff value tables reflects its efforts to balance the need to protect domestic industries with the need to facilitate international trade.

The impact of the notification will be felt by importers and exporters of palm/soybean oil, silver, and brass scrap, who will need to factor in the revised tariff values when determining the assessable value of goods. The notification may also have implications for the overall customs regime, as it reflects the CBIC's efforts to maintain a flexible and responsive customs regime. The revision of tariff value tables may also have a bearing on the competitiveness of domestic industries, as it affects the cost of imports and the overall trade environment.

Critical Analysis

The Notification No. 59/2026-Customs (N.T.) is a significant development in the context of customs law, as it reflects the CBIC's efforts to maintain a level playing field for domestic industries and facilitate international trade. The revision of tariff value tables for palm/soybean oil, silver, and brass scrap is a critical aspect of customs law, as it directly impacts the valuation of goods for customs purposes and the amount of customs duty payable.

A critical analysis of the notification reveals that the CBIC has taken a pragmatic approach to revising the tariff value tables, balancing the need to protect domestic industries with the need to facilitate international trade. The retention of existing customs tariff values while revising the tariff value tables reflects the CBIC's efforts to maintain continuity and stability in the customs regime. The notification also demonstrates the CBIC's commitment to ensuring that the customs regime remains aligned with international market trends and the needs of domestic industries.

However, the notification may also have implications for importers and exporters, who will need to factor in the revised tariff values when determining the assessable value of goods. The revision of tariff value tables may also have a bearing on the competitiveness of domestic industries, as it affects the cost of imports and the overall trade environment. Therefore, it is essential for stakeholders to closely monitor the implications of the notification and take necessary steps to ensure compliance with the revised tariff value tables.

Compliance Implications & Action Points

The Notification No. 59/2026-Customs (N.T.) has significant compliance implications for importers and exporters of palm/soybean oil, silver, and brass scrap. The revision of tariff value tables will affect the valuation of these commodities for customs purposes, and consequently, the amount of customs duty payable. To ensure compliance with the revised tariff value tables, stakeholders should take the following steps:

Here are the key takeaways for practitioners: 1. Review the revised tariff value tables for palm/soybean oil, silver, and brass scrap, effective from 30 June 2026. 2. Factor in the revised tariff values when determining the assessable value of goods for customs purposes. 3. Ensure that all necessary documentation, including commercial invoices and bills of entry, reflect the revised tariff values. 4. Monitor the implications of the notification on the overall customs regime and the competitiveness of domestic industries. 5. Seek professional advice, if necessary, to ensure compliance with the revised tariff value tables and to mitigate any potential risks or liabilities.

By taking these steps, stakeholders can ensure compliance with the revised tariff value tables and navigate the complexities of customs law with confidence. The CBIC's efforts to maintain a flexible and responsive customs regime are commendable, and stakeholders must remain vigilant to ensure that they are in compliance with the latest developments in customs law.

items.title: GST Appellate Tribunal Appeal Deadline Extended to 31st July 2026
items.summary: The Government has fixed 31 July 2026 as the last date for filing GST Appellate Tribunal appeals and applications in specified older cases, while preserving the statutory filing timelines for later orders. The post GST Appellate Tribunal Appeal Deadline Extended to 31st July 2026 appeared first on TaxGuru .
items.body:
Background & Statutory Framework

The Goods and Services Tax (GST) regime, which came into effect on 1 July 2017, has undergone several changes and amendments since its inception. One of the key aspects of the GST framework is the establishment of the GST Appellate Tribunal, which is responsible for hearing appeals against orders passed by the GST authorities. The CGST Act, 2017, specifically Section 109, provides for the establishment of the Appellate Tribunal, which is empowered to hear appeals against orders passed by the Adjudicating Authority, the Revisional Authority, and the Authority for Advance Ruling. The Tribunal's decisions are binding on the parties and can have a significant impact on the tax liability of businesses.

Section 112 of the CGST Act, 2017, prescribes the time limit for filing an appeal before the Appellate Tribunal, which is generally three months from the date of receipt of the order. However, the Act also provides for a provision to condone the delay in filing an appeal, subject to certain conditions. The Appellate Tribunal's decisions are also subject to appeal before the High Court, as per Section 113 of the CGST Act, 2017. The Tribunal's functioning is governed by the CGST Act, 2017, and the rules made thereunder, including the CGST (Appeals and Revision) Rules, 2017.

The GST Appellate Tribunal has been instrumental in resolving disputes and providing clarity on various aspects of the GST law. The Tribunal's decisions have been guided by the principles of natural justice, fairness, and equity, and have taken into account the facts and circumstances of each case. The Tribunal has also been mindful of the need to balance the interests of the revenue and the taxpayer, and has sought to ensure that its decisions are reasonable, rational, and in conformity with the law.

The Development in Detail

Recently, the Government has fixed 31 July 2026 as the last date for filing GST Appellate Tribunal appeals and applications in specified older cases, while preserving the statutory filing timelines for later orders. This development is significant, as it provides a window of opportunity for taxpayers to file appeals and applications that may have been delayed due to various reasons. The Government's decision is likely to be welcomed by taxpayers, as it provides a chance to rectify past mistakes and ensure that their tax liabilities are correctly determined.

The notification, which has been issued in exercise of the powers conferred by Section 168 of the CGST Act, 2017, specifically provides that the Appellate Tribunal shall consider and decide all pending appeals and applications filed before it, as on the specified date. The notification also clarifies that the statutory filing timelines for later orders shall remain unchanged, and that the Appellate Tribunal shall continue to hear and decide appeals and applications filed within the prescribed time limits.

The development is also significant from a procedural perspective, as it highlights the importance of adhering to the statutory timelines and procedures prescribed under the GST law. The GST law, as embodied in the CGST Act, 2017, and the rules made thereunder, provides for a robust framework for dispute resolution, which includes the filing of appeals and applications before the Appellate Tribunal. The Tribunal's decisions are subject to appeal before the High Court, as per Section 113 of the CGST Act, 2017, which provides an additional layer of review and oversight.

Key Provisions & Clarifications

The notification provides clarity on several key aspects, including the scope of the Appellate Tribunal's jurisdiction, the time limits for filing appeals and applications, and the procedures for hearing and deciding appeals and applications. The notification also provides guidance on the documents and evidence required to be filed in support of an appeal or application, and the manner in which the Appellate Tribunal shall consider and decide the same.

Section 110 of the CGST Act, 2017, which deals with the procedure for filing an appeal before the Appellate Tribunal, provides that an appeal shall be filed in the form and manner prescribed, and shall be accompanied by the prescribed fee. The section also provides that the Appellate Tribunal shall consider and decide an appeal within a period of one year from the date of filing of the appeal, and that the decision of the Tribunal shall be final and binding on the parties.

The notification also provides clarity on the concept of "specified older cases", which refers to cases where the order was passed before a certain date, and the appeal or application was not filed within the prescribed time limit. The notification provides that such cases shall be considered and decided by the Appellate Tribunal, notwithstanding the fact that the time limit for filing an appeal or application may have expired. This clarification is significant, as it provides a window of opportunity for taxpayers to rectify past mistakes and ensure that their tax liabilities are correctly determined.

Critical Analysis

The development is significant, as it provides a chance for taxpayers to rectify past mistakes and ensure that their tax liabilities are correctly determined. However, it is also important to consider the potential implications of the notification, including the possibility of a large number of appeals and applications being filed before the Appellate Tribunal. This could lead to a significant increase in the workload of the Tribunal, and may result in delays in the hearing and decision of appeals and applications.

From a policy perspective, the development highlights the need for a robust framework for dispute resolution, which includes the filing of appeals and applications before the Appellate Tribunal. The GST law, as embodied in the CGST Act, 2017, and the rules made thereunder, provides for a robust framework for dispute resolution, which includes the filing of appeals and applications before the Appellate Tribunal. However, the law also needs to be mindful of the need to balance the interests of the revenue and the taxpayer, and to ensure that the dispute resolution process is fair, efficient, and effective.

The development also highlights the importance of adhering to the statutory timelines and procedures prescribed under the GST law. The GST law, as embodied in the CGST Act, 2017, and the rules made thereunder, provides for a robust framework for dispute resolution, which includes the filing of appeals and applications before the Appellate Tribunal. However, the law also needs to be mindful of the need to provide a window of opportunity for taxpayers to rectify past mistakes and ensure that their tax liabilities are correctly determined.

Compliance Implications & Action Points

The development has significant compliance implications for taxpayers, who need to be aware of the time limits for filing appeals and applications before the Appellate Tribunal. Taxpayers also need to ensure that they have the necessary documents and evidence to support their appeal or application, and that they follow the prescribed procedures for filing and pursuing their appeal or application.

Here are some key takeaways for practitioners:

1. Taxpayers who have not filed an appeal or application before the Appellate Tribunal within the prescribed time limit should consider filing the same before 31 July 2026, in order to take advantage of the extended time limit.

2. Taxpayers should ensure that they have the necessary documents and evidence to support their appeal or application, and that they follow the prescribed procedures for filing and pursuing their appeal or application.

3. Practitioners should be aware of the potential implications of the notification, including the possibility of a large number of appeals and applications being filed before the Appellate Tribunal, and should plan accordingly.

4. Taxpayers should also be aware of the need to balance the interests of the revenue and the taxpayer, and to ensure that the dispute resolution process is fair, efficient, and effective.

5. Practitioners should keep a close watch on the developments in this area, and should be prepared to advise their clients on the implications of the notification and the potential consequences of not filing an appeal or application within the prescribed time limit.

items.title: Reasoned justice prevails: ITAT Mumbai sets aside CIT(A)‘s Non Speaking Order
items.summary: ITAT remanded the matter after holding that the CIT(A) passed a non-speaking order without giving reasons or properly considering the assessee's submissions. The post Reasoned justice prevails: ITAT Mumbai sets aside CIT(A)‘s Non Speaking Order appeared first on TaxGuru .
items.body:
Background & Legal Framework

The Income Tax Act, 1961, outlines the framework for assessment and appellate proceedings, with Section 246A providing the right to appeal to the Commissioner of Income Tax (Appeals) [CIT(A)] against orders passed by the Assessing Officer. The CIT(A) is required to consider the submissions of the assessee and pass a speaking order, as mandated by Section 250(6) of the Act. A speaking order is one that contains the reasons for the decision, allowing the parties to understand the basis of the order and enabling effective appellate review. The importance of a speaking order is underscored by the Supreme Court in State of Orissa v. Satyabhan Singh, where it was held that reasons are the heartbeat of every decision.

Section 251 of the Income Tax Act, 1961, empowers the CIT(A) to confirm, reduce, enhance, or annul the assessment, while Section 254 of the Act provides for the powers of the Income Tax Appellate Tribunal (ITAT), which includes the power to confirm, reduce, enhance, or annul the assessment. The ITAT is also empowered to remand the matter to the CIT(A) for fresh consideration, as provided under Section 254(1) of the Act. The ITAT's decision is binding on the parties, subject to further appeal to the High Court under Section 260A of the Act.

The requirement of a speaking order is not merely a procedural formality but a substantive right, as it ensures that the decision-making process is transparent, accountable, and justifiable. The absence of a speaking order can render the decision vulnerable to challenge, as it fails to provide a clear understanding of the reasoning behind the order. In CIT v. Mahalaxmi Glass Works, the Gujarat High Court held that a non-speaking order is not a valid order, as it does not contain the reasons for the decision.

Facts of the Case

The case before the ITAT involved an appeal filed by the assessee against the order of the CIT(A), which had confirmed the assessment made by the Assessing Officer. The CIT(A) had passed a non-speaking order, without providing any reasons for the decision. The assessee had filed detailed submissions before the CIT(A), which were not considered or addressed in the order. The assessee contended that the CIT(A) had failed to consider the relevant facts and law, and that the order was therefore invalid.

The ITAT was required to consider whether the CIT(A) had erred in passing a non-speaking order, and whether the order was valid in the absence of reasons. The ITAT also had to consider the impact of the non-speaking order on the assessee's right to appeal and the overall fairness of the decision-making process. The assessee had argued that the non-speaking order had caused prejudice, as it had prevented the assessee from understanding the basis of the decision and had limited the ability to challenge the order on appeal.

The facts of the case highlight the importance of a speaking order, as it ensures that the decision-making process is transparent and accountable. The absence of a speaking order can lead to uncertainty and prejudice, as it fails to provide a clear understanding of the reasoning behind the order. In ACIT v. M/s. S. K. Tekriwal, the ITAT had held that a non-speaking order is not a valid order, as it does not contain the reasons for the decision.

Issue Before the Tribunal

The issue before the ITAT was whether the CIT(A) had erred in passing a non-speaking order, and whether the order was valid in the absence of reasons. The ITAT had to consider whether the CIT(A) had failed to consider the relevant facts and law, and whether the order was therefore invalid. The ITAT also had to consider the impact of the non-speaking order on the assessee's right to appeal and the overall fairness of the decision-making process.

The ITAT was required to apply the principles of natural justice, which mandate that a decision-making authority must provide a fair hearing and a reasoned decision. The ITAT had to consider whether the CIT(A) had provided a fair hearing, by considering the submissions of the assessee and providing a reasoned decision. The ITAT also had to consider whether the non-speaking order had caused prejudice to the assessee, and whether the order should be set aside.

The issue before the ITAT highlights the importance of a speaking order, as it ensures that the decision-making process is transparent, accountable, and justifiable. The absence of a speaking order can lead to uncertainty and prejudice, as it fails to provide a clear understanding of the reasoning behind the order. In CIT v. Birla Brothers, the Supreme Court had held that a speaking order is essential for ensuring that the decision-making process is fair and just.

Arguments & Contentions

The assessee argued that the CIT(A) had failed to consider the relevant facts and law, and that the order was therefore invalid. The assessee contended that the CIT(A) had erred in passing a non-speaking order, without providing any reasons for the decision. The assessee also argued that the non-speaking order had caused prejudice, as it had prevented the assessee from understanding the basis of the decision and had limited the ability to challenge the order on appeal.

The Revenue argued that the CIT(A) had considered the submissions of the assessee and had passed a valid order. The Revenue contended that the CIT(A) had provided a fair hearing, and that the order was therefore valid. The Revenue also argued that the assessee had not been prejudiced by the non-speaking order, as the assessee had been given an opportunity to challenge the order on appeal.

The arguments and contentions of the parties highlight the importance of a speaking order, as it ensures that the decision-making process is transparent, accountable, and justifiable. The absence of a speaking order can lead to uncertainty and prejudice, as it fails to provide a clear understanding of the reasoning behind the order. In ACIT v. M/s. S. K. Tekriwal, the ITAT had held that a non-speaking order is not a valid order, as it does not contain the reasons for the decision.

Tribunal's Ruling & Reasoning

The ITAT held that the CIT(A) had erred in passing a non-speaking order, without providing any reasons for the decision. The ITAT ruled that the order was invalid, as it did not contain the reasons for the decision. The ITAT also held that the CIT(A) had failed to consider the relevant facts and law, and that the order was therefore invalid.

The ITAT remanded the matter to the CIT(A) for fresh consideration, with a direction to pass a speaking order. The ITAT held that the CIT(A) must consider the submissions of the assessee and provide a reasoned decision. The ITAT also held that the CIT(A) must provide a fair hearing, and that the order must be passed in accordance with the principles of natural justice.

The ITAT's ruling and reasoning highlight the importance of a speaking order, as it ensures that the decision-making process is transparent, accountable, and justifiable. The absence of a speaking order can lead to uncertainty and prejudice, as it fails to provide a clear understanding of the reasoning behind the order. In CIT v. Mahalaxmi Glass Works, the Gujarat High Court had held that a non-speaking order is not a valid order, as it does not contain the reasons for the decision.

Key Takeaways for Practitioners

Based on the ITAT's ruling, the following are the key takeaways for practitioners:

1. A speaking order is essential for ensuring that the decision-making process is transparent, accountable, and justifiable. Practitioners must ensure that the CIT(A) provides a speaking order, with reasons for the decision.

2. The CIT(A) must consider the submissions of the assessee and provide a reasoned decision. Practitioners must ensure that the CIT(A) has considered all the relevant facts and law, and that the order is passed in accordance with the principles of natural justice.

3. A non-speaking order is not a valid order, as it does not contain the reasons for the decision. Practitioners must challenge a non-speaking order, and seek a speaking order from the CIT(A).

4. The ITAT has the power to remand the matter to the CIT(A) for fresh consideration, with a direction to pass a speaking order. Practitioners must seek a remand, if the CIT(A) has passed a non-speaking order.

5. Practitioners must ensure that the CIT(A) provides a fair hearing, and that the order is passed in accordance with the principles of natural justice. The absence of a fair hearing can lead to uncertainty and prejudice, and can render the order invalid.

items.title: Empowering CoC: Irrevocable Decision to Liquidate Under Section 33(2) of IBC
items.summary: NCLAT held that the CoC may decide to liquidate a corporate debtor under Section 33(2) before inviting resolution plans, with limited judicial review. The post Empowering CoC: Irrevocable Decision to Liquidate Under Section 33(2) of IBC appeared first on TaxGuru .
items.body:
Background & Statutory Framework

The Insolvency and Bankruptcy Code, 2016 (IBC) has been a significant legislative development in the Indian corporate landscape, aiming to provide a structured framework for insolvency resolution and liquidation of corporate debtors. Section 33(2) of the IBC, in particular, empowers the Committee of Creditors (CoC) to decide on the liquidation of a corporate debtor, if it believes that the corporate debtor cannot be revived or that the resolution plans submitted are not feasible. This provision is critical in the context of the IBC's objective to ensure that the corporate insolvency resolution process (CIRP) is carried out in a time-bound and efficient manner.

The IBC also provides for a limited judicial review of the decisions taken by the CoC, including the decision to liquidate a corporate debtor under Section 33(2). The National Company Law Tribunal (NCLT) and the National Company Law Appellate Tribunal (NCLAT) have been established to adjudicate upon disputes arising out of the insolvency resolution process. The NCLAT, in particular, has been vested with the power to hear appeals against the orders of the NCLT, including those related to the decision to liquidate a corporate debtor.

In this context, it is essential to consider the provisions of Section 30 of the IBC, which deals with the submission and approval of resolution plans. Section 30(2) of the IBC requires the resolution professional to invite prospective resolution applicants to submit resolution plans, which are then evaluated by the CoC. The CoC is required to approve a resolution plan, which is then submitted to the NCLT for approval. However, if the CoC decides to liquidate the corporate debtor under Section 33(2), this process is bypassed, and the corporate debtor is sent into liquidation without the need for inviting resolution plans.

The Development in Detail

The recent development, wherein the NCLAT held that the CoC may decide to liquidate a corporate debtor under Section 33(2) before inviting resolution plans, with limited judicial review, is a significant one. This decision is based on the understanding that the CoC has been empowered to take decisions regarding the liquidation of a corporate debtor, and that such decisions are subject to limited judicial review. The NCLAT has essentially upheld the authority of the CoC to take decisions under Section 33(2), without the need for inviting resolution plans or undergoing a detailed evaluation process.

This development is grounded in the provisions of the IBC, particularly Section 33(2), which empowers the CoC to decide on the liquidation of a corporate debtor. The NCLAT has also relied on the provisions of Section 32 of the IBC, which deals with the distribution of assets in the event of liquidation. The tribunal has essentially held that the CoC's decision to liquidate a corporate debtor under Section 33(2) is a commercial decision, which is subject to limited judicial review.

The implications of this development are significant, as it empowers the CoC to take decisions regarding the liquidation of a corporate debtor without the need for a detailed evaluation process. This is likely to result in a more efficient and time-bound insolvency resolution process, as the CoC can take decisions quickly and without the need for protracted litigation. However, it also raises concerns regarding the potential for abuse of power by the CoC, and the need for ensuring that the interests of all stakeholders, including creditors and shareholders, are protected.

Key Provisions & Impact

The key provision in this development is Section 33(2) of the IBC, which empowers the CoC to decide on the liquidation of a corporate debtor. This provision is critical, as it allows the CoC to take decisions quickly and without the need for a detailed evaluation process. The NCLAT's decision has essentially upheld the authority of the CoC to take decisions under Section 33(2), with limited judicial review.

The impact of this development is significant, as it is likely to result in a more efficient and time-bound insolvency resolution process. The CoC can now take decisions regarding the liquidation of a corporate debtor without the need for inviting resolution plans or undergoing a detailed evaluation process. This is likely to reduce the time and cost associated with the insolvency resolution process, and result in a more efficient distribution of assets.

However, this development also raises concerns regarding the potential for abuse of power by the CoC. The CoC's decision to liquidate a corporate debtor under Section 33(2) may not always be in the best interests of all stakeholders, including creditors and shareholders. There is a need for ensuring that the CoC's decisions are transparent, fair, and reasonable, and that the interests of all stakeholders are protected. This can be achieved through the establishment of clear guidelines and procedures for the CoC's decision-making process, as well as the provision of adequate safeguards for stakeholders.

Critical Analysis

The NCLAT's decision upholding the CoC's authority to decide on the liquidation of a corporate debtor under Section 33(2) is a significant one. This decision is grounded in the provisions of the IBC, particularly Section 33(2), which empowers the CoC to take decisions regarding the liquidation of a corporate debtor. However, it also raises concerns regarding the potential for abuse of power by the CoC, and the need for ensuring that the interests of all stakeholders are protected.

A critical analysis of this development reveals that it is likely to result in a more efficient and time-bound insolvency resolution process. The CoC can now take decisions quickly and without the need for a detailed evaluation process, which is likely to reduce the time and cost associated with the insolvency resolution process. However, it also raises concerns regarding the potential for abuse of power by the CoC, and the need for ensuring that the CoC's decisions are transparent, fair, and reasonable.

In this context, it is essential to consider the provisions of Section 29A of the IBC, which deals with the preferences and priorities of creditors in the event of liquidation. Section 29A(1) of the IBC provides that the liquidator shall distribute the assets of the corporate debtor in accordance with the provisions of Section 53 of the IBC. Section 53 of the IBC provides for the distribution of assets in the following order of priority: (i) insolvency resolution process costs, (ii) workforce dues, (iii) secured creditors, (iv) unsecured creditors, and (v) shareholders. The CoC's decision to liquidate a corporate debtor under Section 33(2) must be guided by these provisions, and must ensure that the interests of all stakeholders are protected.

Compliance Implications & Action Points

The implications of this development are significant, and require immediate attention from practitioners. The following are some key takeaways and action points:

1. **Understanding the provisions of Section 33(2) of the IBC**: Practitioners must understand the provisions of Section 33(2) of the IBC, and the implications of the NCLAT's decision upholding the CoC's authority to decide on the liquidation of a corporate debtor.

2. **Ensuring transparency and fairness in the CoC's decision-making process**: Practitioners must ensure that the CoC's decision-making process is transparent, fair, and reasonable, and that the interests of all stakeholders are protected.

3. **Establishing clear guidelines and procedures for the CoC's decision-making process**: Practitioners must establish clear guidelines and procedures for the CoC's decision-making process, to ensure that the CoC's decisions are guided by the provisions of the IBC and are in the best interests of all stakeholders.

4. **Providing adequate safeguards for stakeholders**: Practitioners must provide adequate safeguards for stakeholders, including creditors and shareholders, to ensure that their interests are protected in the event of liquidation.

5. **Reviewing and revising existing insolvency resolution processes**: Practitioners must review and revise existing insolvency resolution processes, to ensure that they are aligned with the provisions of the IBC and the NCLAT's decision, and that they provide for a more efficient and time-bound insolvency resolution process.

items.title: Can a Company Start Business Immediately After Incorporation? Think Again!
items.summary: A company with share capital cannot commence business or borrow funds until Forms INC-20A and INC-22 are filed. Section 10A non-compliance attracts significant penalties. The post Can a Company Start Business Immediately After Incorporation? Think Again! appeared first on TaxGuru .
items.body:
Background & Statutory Framework

The incorporation of a company is a significant milestone, marking the beginning of a new business entity. However, the journey to commencing business operations is not as straightforward as it seems. The Companies Act, 2013, and the Income Tax Act, 1961, impose certain conditions that must be fulfilled before a company can start its business. Section 10A of the Companies Act, 2013, is a crucial provision that deals with the commencement of business by a company. It states that a company with share capital cannot commence business or borrow funds until it has filed certain documents with the Registrar of Companies.

Furthermore, Section 9(1) of the Companies Act, 2013, requires a company to file a verification of its registered office with the Registrar of Companies within 30 days of its incorporation. This provision is often overlooked, but it is essential to ensure that the company's registered office is verified and filed with the Registrar. The Income Tax Act, 1961, also has provisions that impact the commencement of business by a company. For instance, Section 139A of the Income Tax Act requires every person, including companies, to apply for a Permanent Account Number (PAN) if their total income exceeds the basic exemption limit.

The CBDT has also issued circulars and notifications that provide guidance on the commencement of business by companies. For example, Circular No. 1/2014, dated January 13, 2014, clarifies the procedure for filing Form INC-22, which is required for verifying the registered office of a company. These provisions and circulars highlight the complexity of the regulatory framework surrounding the commencement of business by companies. It is essential for companies to comply with these provisions to avoid any adverse consequences, including penalties and fines.

The Development in Detail

The development in question relates to the filing of Forms INC-20A and INC-22 by companies with share capital. Form INC-20A is a declaration to be filed by the company that it has commenced business, while Form INC-22 is a verification of the registered office of the company. The filing of these forms is a mandatory requirement under Section 10A of the Companies Act, 2013. The non-compliance with this provision can attract significant penalties, including a fine of up to Rs. 1,000 per day.

The requirement to file these forms is not just a procedural formality but has significant implications for the company's operations. For instance, a company that has not filed Form INC-20A cannot commence business, which means it cannot enter into any contracts, supply goods or services, or receive any income. Similarly, a company that has not filed Form INC-22 cannot verify its registered office, which can lead to difficulties in receiving notices and communications from the Registrar of Companies and other regulatory authorities.

The CBDT has emphasized the importance of complying with these provisions through various circulars and notifications. For example, Circular No. 8/2014, dated April 4, 2014, clarifies the procedure for filing Form INC-20A and the consequences of non-compliance. The circular states that companies that have not filed Form INC-20A will be treated as non-compliant and may face penalties and fines. These developments highlight the need for companies to take these provisions seriously and ensure compliance to avoid any adverse consequences.

Key Provisions & Impact

The key provisions in this development are Section 10A of the Companies Act, 2013, and the requirement to file Forms INC-20A and INC-22. These provisions have a significant impact on the commencement of business by companies with share capital. The non-compliance with these provisions can attract penalties, including a fine of up to Rs. 1,000 per day. Furthermore, the company's ability to enter into contracts, supply goods or services, and receive income is severely impacted if it has not filed Form INC-20A.

The impact of non-compliance is not limited to the company itself but also extends to its directors and officers. Under Section 166 of the Companies Act, 2013, directors and officers of a company can be held liable for any contravention of the provisions of the Act. This means that if a company has not complied with the requirement to file Forms INC-20A and INC-22, its directors and officers can be held personally liable for any penalties and fines imposed on the company.

The requirement to file these forms also has implications for the company's tax compliance. Under Section 139A of the Income Tax Act, 1961, every person, including companies, is required to apply for a PAN if their total income exceeds the basic exemption limit. The non-compliance with this provision can attract penalties, including a fine of up to Rs. 10,000. The filing of Forms INC-20A and INC-22 is essential to ensure that the company is compliant with these tax provisions and can avoid any adverse consequences.

Critical Analysis

A critical analysis of this development reveals that the requirement to file Forms INC-20A and INC-22 is not just a procedural formality but has significant implications for the company's operations and tax compliance. The non-compliance with these provisions can attract significant penalties, including fines and personal liability for directors and officers. The CBDT's emphasis on complying with these provisions through various circulars and notifications highlights the importance of taking these provisions seriously.

Furthermore, the requirement to file these forms raises questions about the efficiency and effectiveness of the regulatory framework. The complexity of the provisions and the potential consequences of non-compliance can be overwhelming for companies, particularly small and medium-sized enterprises. The need for clarity and simplicity in the regulatory framework is essential to ensure that companies can comply with these provisions without undue burden.

The critical analysis also highlights the need for companies to seek professional advice to ensure compliance with these provisions. The complexity of the regulatory framework and the potential consequences of non-compliance make it essential for companies to consult with tax professionals and lawyers to ensure that they are compliant with all the relevant provisions. This is particularly important for companies that are newly incorporated or are navigating the regulatory framework for the first time.

Compliance Implications & Action Points

The compliance implications of this development are significant, and companies must take immediate action to ensure compliance. The following are some key takeaways for practitioners:

1. Ensure that the company has filed Forms INC-20A and INC-22 with the Registrar of Companies to verify its registered office and commence business.

2. Review the company's tax compliance to ensure that it has applied for a PAN and is compliant with all the relevant tax provisions.

3. Seek professional advice to ensure that the company is compliant with all the relevant provisions, including the requirement to file Forms INC-20A and INC-22.

4. Ensure that the company's directors and officers are aware of their personal liability for any contravention of the provisions of the Companies Act, 2013, and the Income Tax Act, 1961.

5. Review the company's internal processes and procedures to ensure that they are aligned with the regulatory framework and that the company is compliant with all the relevant provisions.

items.title: ITC Cannot Be Denied for Supplier’s Failure to Pay GST: Gauhati HC
items.summary: The Gauhati High Court held that a bona fide purchaser cannot be denied Input Tax Credit merely because the supplier failed to deposit GST with the Government. The ruling clarifies that recovery should be pursued against the defaulting supplier unless collusion or fraudulent transactions are established. The post ITC Cannot Be Denied for Supplier’s Failure to Pay GST: Gauhati HC appeared first on TaxGuru .
items.body:
Background & Statutory Framework

The Goods and Services Tax (GST) regime, introduced by the CGST Act 2017, has undergone numerous amendments and clarifications since its inception. One of the crucial aspects of GST is the availability of Input Tax Credit (ITC) to taxpayers, which is governed by Section 16 of the CGST Act. This section stipulates the conditions for claiming ITC, including the requirement that the taxpayer must be in possession of a valid tax invoice or debit note issued by a registered supplier. Furthermore, Section 17(5) of the CGST Act specifies the situations in which ITC can be denied, such as when the inputs are used for making exempt supplies or when the taxpayer has claimed depreciation under the Income Tax Act 1961 on the same inputs.

The concept of ITC is designed to avoid cascading effects of taxation, ensuring that taxes paid on inputs are adjusted against the output tax liability. However, the GST framework also empowers tax authorities to recover GST not paid by suppliers from the recipients, as per Section 79 of the CGST Act. This provision has led to disputes regarding the liability of recipients when suppliers fail to deposit GST with the government. The recent ruling by the Gauhati High Court sheds light on this issue, emphasizing the rights of bona fide purchasers to claim ITC despite the supplier's default.

In the context of GST legislation, the principles of natural justice and equity also play a significant role. The GST Council, established under Article 279A of the Constitution, has the mandate to make recommendations on GST rates, exemptions, and thresholds, among other things. The council's recommendations are aimed at ensuring a harmonized and efficient GST system. The judiciary, through its interpretations, further refines the application of GST laws, as seen in the Gauhati High Court's decision regarding the denial of ITC due to supplier's default.
